text.content:
When you need something big from an important NPC — military support, a legendary artifact, a pardon, crucial intelligence — the Director may call for a negotiation. Think of it as a structured conversation where your arguments, roleplaying, and strategy all matter.

When Does Negotiation Happen?

Negotiations occur when an NPC is conflicted — they have reason to help you, but also reason to refuse. A simple request (one test) or a threat of violence is not a negotiation. Negotiation is for high-stakes asks that could change the course of the adventure.

Your party must willingly start the negotiation. You always have the right to walk away.

What you cannot do: Negotiation is not mind control. You can not convince an NPC to act against their core nature, hand over everything they own, or do something they would never seriously consider. You can convince them that helping you aligns with who they already are.

What the NPC Tracks

During a negotiation, the Director tracks two key numbers for the NPC:

Stat What It Means
Interest (0–5) How eager the NPC is to make a deal with you. Higher is better. At 5, you get the best possible offer. At 0, they refuse and may become hostile.
Patience (0–5) How long the NPC will keep talking. Arguments spend patience. When it hits 0, whatever they are offering is their final offer — take it or leave it.

NPCs also have hidden motivations and pitfalls that dramatically affect how your arguments land.

Making Arguments

The core of negotiation is making arguments — reasoned appeals to convince the NPC that helping you is in their interest. One hero speaks for the group each time, though you can discuss strategy out of character beforehand.

Every argument needs two things:

  1. A claim — what you want the NPC to believe or do.
  2. A justification — why they should believe it or do it.

Example:

Half an argument: Helping us defeat Lord Saxton is good for you in the long run.

Full argument: Helping us defeat Lord Saxton is good for you in the long run, because we know he is coming for your kingdom after Bedegar falls!

After you make your argument, you roll a test — usually Reason, Intuition, or Presence with an applicable skill. The NPC then responds and makes an offer based on their current interest level.

Motivations and Pitfalls

Every NPC in a negotiation has motivations (topics they respond well to) and pitfalls (topics that anger or alienate them). These are two sides of the same coin — what motivates one NPC might be a pitfall for another.

If Your Argument Hits… What Happens
A Motivation The test is easier (medium difficulty). Each motivation can only be successfully appealed to once.
Neither The test is harder. You can still succeed, but it costs more patience.
A Pitfall The argument automatically fails. The NPC loses interest and patience. No test is made.

The twelve possible motivation/pitfall types are: Benevolence, Discovery, Freedom, Greed, Higher Authority, Justice, Legacy, Peace, Power, Protection, Revelry, and Vengeance.

Discovering What Matters to the NPC

You will not always know what motivates or offends an NPC. Here are your options:

Method How It Works
Just ask Ask the NPC what they want. They may hint at or reveal a motivation, especially if they are already somewhat interested.
Read them Make a Reason, Intuition, or Presence test during the negotiation. On a strong success, learn one motivation or pitfall (your choice). On a poor result, the NPC notices and patience decreases.
Do your homework Research the NPC before the negotiation. Read their diary, talk to their associates, investigate their reputation. No negotiation test needed — you walk in prepared.

Limit: After attempting to read an NPC, you cannot try again until you make an argument or the negotiation ends.

What the NPC Offers

After each argument, the NPC responds with an offer (or a refusal) based on their current interest. Their body language and words should give you a clear sense of how things are going:

NPC Response What It Means
Positive Your argument increased their interest. They engage, nod, consider your point.
Negative Your argument decreased their interest. They push back, disagree, or look offended.
Impatient Interest did not change. They sigh, tap fingers, ask for something real. Time is running out.

The better the offer, the closer you are to what you asked for. The NPC might offer everything and more, agree to your terms, agree but ask for something extra, offer something lesser, or refuse outright. If things go badly enough, they may end the negotiation entirely and become an enemy.

When to Push and When to Stop

After each offer, your party decides: accept what is on the table, or make another argument to try for a better deal. You can also walk away from any negotiation at any time.

The negotiation ends when:

  • The NPC runs out of patience (their current offer is final).
  • Interest hits the maximum (you get the best deal possible).
  • Interest hits zero (the NPC refuses and may become hostile).
  • You accept the current offer.
  • You walk away.

Renown Matters

If an NPC has heard of you and respects (or fears) your reputation, your Renown can give you an edge during negotiation. A famous hero gains an advantage when making charming or inspiring arguments. An infamous hero gains an advantage when making intimidating or boastful arguments.

Everyone Can Participate

Arguments can use Reason, Intuition, or Presence with any creatively applied skill. You do not need to be the party face to contribute:

  • A tactician might use Reason + Lead to lay out a logical battle plan.
  • A conduit might use Intuition + Read Person to uncover hidden motives.
  • A shadow might use Reason + Criminal Underworld to reveal intel about the NPC's enemies.
  • A fury might use Presence + Intimidate to impress upon the NPC the gravity of the situation.

Discuss strategy as a group, play to your strengths, and take turns making the arguments where each hero shines.

Tips for Players

  • Investigate first. Learning motivations and pitfalls before the negotiation starts gives you a huge advantage and costs no patience.
  • Do not waste motivations. Each one can only be appealed to once. Save them for your strongest arguments.
  • Avoid pitfalls. A pitfall costs you interest and patience with no test. Read the room, and if the NPC warns you off a topic, listen.
  • Arguments need substance. Do not just say what you want — explain why it benefits the NPC.
  • Know when to stop. A decent offer now may be better than pushing for a great offer and watching the NPC's patience run out.
  • Threats end negotiations. Explicit threats of violence exit the negotiation framework. Any compliance gained through threats is temporary.
